narrative:
First Warn Forecast
Tuesday, June 9, 2026

Headlines
Dangerous heat and humidity continue through Thursday with heat index values reaching 100 to 105 degrees.
Severe thunderstorms are possible late Wednesday into Wednesday night, especially across northeast Missouri and west-central Illinois.
A more widespread severe weather threat is expected Thursday afternoon and evening with damaging winds, large hail, and tornadoes all possible.

The Mid-Mississippi Valley remains firmly in the grip of early summer heat and humidity today. Afternoon temperatures will climb into the lower 90s across much of Missouri and Illinois, while dew points in the 70s will push heat index values into the 100 to 105 degree range. A Heat Advisory remains in effect for portions of the area where confidence is highest that dangerous heat levels will be reached. Anyone spending extended time outdoors should take frequent breaks, stay hydrated, and avoid strenuous activities during the hottest part of the day.

A few showers and thunderstorms may linger this morning and early afternoon, mainly south and east of St. Louis, as the remnants of overnight storms move through the region. While widespread severe weather is not expected today, an isolated storm could produce gusty winds and heavy rainfall. Most locations, however, will experience hot and humid conditions with at least partial sunshine.

Attention then turns to a developing severe weather threat beginning Wednesday afternoon and evening. Thunderstorms are expected to develop northwest of the region and move southeast toward portions of northeast Missouri and west-central Illinois. The exact evolution of these storms remains uncertain and will play a major role in determining the overall threat.

If storms develop earlier in the afternoon and farther northwest, they may organize into a line before reaching the area. In that scenario, damaging straight-line winds would become the primary threat, with a secondary risk for isolated tornadoes. However, if storms develop later in the day and closer to the region, they could remain more discrete and intense. This would increase the potential for all severe weather hazards, including damaging winds, large hail, and tornadoes.

The severe weather threat becomes even more concerning on Thursday as a cold front approaches from the west. Confidence remains lower than average regarding the exact timing and location of the strongest storms because much will depend on how Wednesday night's storms evolve. However, a reasonable worst-case scenario would involve numerous thunderstorms developing across the region Thursday afternoon and evening in an environment favorable for severe weather.

Should this scenario unfold, thunderstorms would be capable of producing all modes of severe weather, including damaging wind gusts, large hail, and tornadoes. Residents across Missouri and Illinois should closely monitor forecasts over the next 24 to 48 hours as details become clearer.

The good news is that this cold front will eventually bring relief from the heat. High temperatures are expected to fall back into the 80s by Friday and through much of the upcoming weekend. While additional chances for showers and thunderstorms remain in the forecast at times, the oppressive heat and humidity currently affecting the region will begin to ease.

As always, now is the time to review your severe weather safety plan, ensure you have multiple ways to receive warnings, and prepare for rapidly changing weather conditions later this week.

First. Accurate. Trusted.
